stem: How many solutions in nonnegative integers are there to the equation \( \sum_{i=1}^{[[k]]} x_i = [[n]] \)? Solutions that differ in any coordinate are counted separately.
solution: By stars and bars the number of nonnegative solutions is \( \binom{[[n]] + [[k]] - 1}{[[k]] - 1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \binom{n+k-1}{k} \), placing the bars in the wrong count of gaps.
This is \( \binom{n-1}{k-1} \), the count of strictly POSITIVE solutions.
This shifts the top of the binomial up by one.
This uses \( k-2 \) bars instead of \( k-1 \).
This is off from the correct count by [[k]].
This is off from the correct count by [[k]].

stem: How many solutions in POSITIVE integers (each \( x_i \ge 1 \)) are there to the equation \( \sum_{i=1}^{[[k]]} x_i = [[n]] \)?
solution: Give each \( x_i \) its required unit first, leaving \( [[n]]-[[k]] \) to distribute freely among [[k]] nonnegative slack variables: \( \binom{([[n]]-[[k]])+[[k]]-1}{[[k]]-1} = \binom{[[n]]-1}{[[k]]-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the count of NONNEGATIVE solutions, forgetting the positivity requirement.
This uses the wrong lower index \( k \) instead of \( k-1 \).
This subtracts [[k]] again after already accounting for positivity, an extra unwarranted shift.
This shifts the top of the binomial up instead of down.
This is off from the correct count by [[k]].
This is off from the correct count by [[k]].

stem: How many solutions in nonnegative integers are there to the INEQUALITY \( \sum_{i=1}^{[[k]]} x_i \le [[n]] \)?
solution: Introduce a nonnegative slack variable \( x_{[[k]]+1} = [[n]] - \sum x_i \) to turn the inequality into the equality \( x_1+\cdots+x_{[[k]]}+x_{[[k]]+1} = [[n]] \) in \( [[k]]+1 \) variables: \( \binom{[[n]]+[[k]]}{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This applies the equality-case formula directly, forgetting the extra slack variable needed for the inequality.
This uses the wrong lower index \( k-1 \) instead of \( k \).
This shifts the top of the binomial up by one too many.
This forgets the slack variable entirely, using only the original [[k]] variables.
This is off from the correct count by [[k]].
This is off from the correct count by [[k]].

stem: How many distinct monomial terms \( x_1^{e_1}x_2^{e_2}\cdots x_{[[k]]}^{e_{[[k]]}} \) of total degree exactly [[n]] are there in [[k]] variables (that is, how many choices of nonnegative-integer exponents \( e_1,\dots,e_{[[k]]} \) satisfy \( e_1+\cdots+e_{[[k]]}=[[n]] \))?
solution: Each monomial of degree [[n]] corresponds to a nonnegative-integer solution of \( e_1+\cdots+e_{[[k]]}=[[n]] \); by stars and bars there are \( \binom{[[n]]+[[k]]-1}{[[k]]-1} = [[answer]] \) such monomials.
distractor_rationales: This uses the inequality-case (degree AT MOST n) formula instead of exact degree n.
This uses the POSITIVE-exponent formula, forgetting that an exponent may be 0.
This treats the exponent assignment as an ordered permutation-style count.
This treats each of the [[n]] degree units as independently choosing one of [[k]] variables without accounting for the shared total correctly.
This is off from the correct count by [[k]].
This is off from the correct count by [[k]].
